title: Feature: buildah-push rkt support
body:
Thanks for all the effort on Buildah, and for making it open source.

**Description**
Rkt has the most elegant discovery mechanism.  It eliminates the need for container registry infrastructure/headaches, whether in public or private clouds.

I don't think it will go away anytime soon.

[woofwoofinc/docker2aci](https://github.com/woofwoofinc/docker2aci) has a working example of the code required to convert from oci to aci.

It would be nice to do this, see [rkt's discovery description](https://github.com/appc/spec/blob/master/spec/discovery.md), where rkt-version -> oci-tag:

````bash
signing_key=/path/to/key.asc
buildah push --rkt-key=${signing_key} ${OCI_NAME}:${OCI_TAG} 
#produces {name}-{version}.aci and {name}-{version}.aci.asc and {name}-{version}-public.gpg
buildah push --rkt-key=${signing_key} --rkt-os=linux --rkt-arch=amd64 ${OCI_NAME}:${OCI_TAG} 
#produces {name}-{version}-{os}-{arch}.aci and {name}-{version}-{os}-{arch}.aci.asc and {name}-{version}-public.gpg
````

comments.body:
\*shrug\* If someone wanted to do the work to implement an ACI transport for c/image, I don’t see a reason to refuse such a contribution.

- There would probably be a performance cost in unpacking and re-packing the tarballs, but docker2aci is paying the same cost.
- ACI does not support image layering, which makes the cost more or less unavoidable, at least for a c/image backend; c/image only understands images to the level of layers. (There already is a precedent, in the OSTree transport, for unpacking the layer tarballs.)  Alternatively, it might be more efficient to write a straight buildah→ACI output implementation, generating the tarball with the `rootfs` directory directly from c/storage—more efficient, but not at all reusable.
- I’m less sure about a nice generic way to incorporate the signing, but it’s easy to imagine a hack that works (as the example shows, teaching `buildah` etc. about the RKT keys specially).
- It’s not obvious that that work could reuse that much of docker2aci, but I have only spent about 2 minutes checking. (Of course, a shell script running buildah+docker2aci should be easy; but also much less efficient than native c/image integration.)

Overall, though, given https://github.com/rkt/rkt/blob/master/ROADMAP.md saying that OCI “will become the preferred way over appc” it seems hard to recommend that anyone should take on the work to implement such a backend—especially if the point is discovery&distribution rather than the image format. @vbatts can probably give much more insight about the viability and status of the standards and projects, though.


For simplifying the distribution of OCI/Docker images, instead of throwing the OCI infrastructure out wholesale, I’d suggest just focusing on the distribution problem directly.

For example, AFAICS the docker/distribution protocol, for shipping read-only images with no online update facility, can be backed by an ordinary static HTTP server (it only really needs to set `Content-Type` and `Content-Length` correctly). So, it should be possible to write a configuration for an ordinary HTTP server to serve as a docker/distribution server, and to write a (very simple) containers/image transport that generates the static file layout serving images in such a server. 

Would that be interesting WRT “eliminating the need for container registry infrastructure/headaches”, or am I missing the mark entirely?
Is this to facilitate a migration?

buildah is already _very_ similar workflow to the now deprecated 
`acbuild` tool. I'm feeling like a rkt build workflow would be easier to 
just import from an OCI image into rkt, and let rkt do its translation 
into an aci image on the back-end.


On 04/09/2018 10:37 AM, Miloslav Trmač wrote:
>
> *shrug* If someone wanted to do the work to implement an ACI transport 
> for c/image, I don’t see a reason to refuse such a contribution.
>
>   * There would probably be a performance cost in unpacking and
>     re-packing the tarballs, but docker2aci is paying the same cost.
>   * ACI does not support image layering, which makes the cost more or
>     less unavoidable, at least for a c/image backend; c/image only
>     understands images to the level of layers. (There already is a
>     precedent, in the OSTree transport, for unpacking the layer
>     tarballs.) Alternatively, it might be more efficient to write a
>     straight buildah→ACI output implementation, generating the tarball
>     with the |rootfs| directory directly from c/storage—more
>     efficient, but not at all reusable.
>   * I’m less sure about a nice generic way to incorporate the signing,
>     but it’s easy to imagine a hack that works (as the example shows,
>     teaching |buildah| etc. about the RKT keys specially).
>   * It’s not obvious that that work could reuse that much of
>     docker2aci, but I have only spent about 2 minutes checking. (Of
>     course, a shell script running buildah+docker2aci should be easy;
>     but also much less efficient than native c/image integration.)
>
> Overall, though, given 
> https://github.com/rkt/rkt/blob/master/ROADMAP.md saying that OCI 
> “will become the preferred way over appc” it seems hard to recommend 
> that anyone should take on the work to implement such a 
> backend—especially if the point is discovery&distribution rather than 
> the image format. @vbatts <https://github.com/vbatts> can probably 
> give much more insight about the viability and status of the standards 
> and projects, though.
>
> For simplifying the distribution of OCI/Docker images, instead of 
> throwing the OCI infrastructure out wholesale, I’d suggest just 
> focusing on the distribution problem directly.
>
> For example, AFAICS the docker/distribution format, for shipping 
> read-only images with no online update facility, can be backed by an 
> ordinary static HTTP server (it only really needs to set 
> |Content-Type| and |Content-Length| correctly). So, it should be 
> possible to write a configuration for an ordinary HTTP server to serve 
> as a docker/distribution server, and to write a (very simple) 
> containers/image transport that generates the static file layout 
> serving images in such a server.
>
> Would that be interesting WRT “eliminating the need for container 
> registry infrastructure/headaches”, or am I missing the mark entirely?
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub 
> <https://github.com/projectatomic/buildah/issues/563#issuecomment-379774751>, 
> or mute the thread 
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AAEF6UhcWnT2RJLbh40nxfTjmfCMI0ZJks5tm3JAgaJpZM4TLsjs>.
>

All valid points.  I don't know of the docker spec so can't comment about the docker discovery/distribution mechanism.   
In my expereince the rkt spec is the simplest possible - right now `rkt fetch taqtiqa.io/....` uses a Route53, AWS-S3 routing rules to point to github release files - simple. There is a 2GB github-ceiling in this approach.

There are workarounds, so closing.

Actually, I'd like to keep this open.  As @mtrmac indicates, such functionality isn't opposed.  He's correct the appc indicates the OCI spec are preferred. But.... that spec isn't finished.  Its not yet clear to me its an improvement, i.e. I should migrate from rkt.

The appc Roadmap page also indicates rkt:
- will continue to support the current discovery protocol/mechanism.
- version 1 deprecation isn't contemplated

I've tried to read the [OCI distribution spec](https://github.com/opencontainers/distribution-spec/blob/master/spec.md
) - ouch.  All that to download a zip file?  It seems trust, identity, and provenance models are being intermingled and that's causing the pain.

I won't pretend to even partly understand it.  I can say this. In a couple of hours I read and understood the appc discovery mechanism, and worked out a project could distribute signed opensource containers **without writing any code** - just set some configuration values on Amazon's and github's services and you're good to go:  Opensource projects with container distribution infrastructure superior to DockerHub.  Not bad.

That is a nontrivial point for opensource projects.  

All the above is by way of making the case that it would be nice to have a build tool that considered `rkt` v1 a first class citizen.

Thanks for considering.

[updated]: Sorry @vbatts omitted to answer you question. Is this to facilitate a migration? Yes.  From OCI to RKT. It is invaluable to have a working alternative in place in case Docker or OCI goes rogue again.

@vbatts  of course everything you say is correct.  Buildah uses exactly the same pattern as acbuild.  
Rkt does allow you to convert an OCI image. Further there are workarounds to convert an OCI image using docker2aci.  Much that same way that skopeo allows you to download images, so does buildah.

It would be nice to have a simpler toolbox for building images.  Distributing them is another issue, although Buildah does help with that too for docker users.  It would similarly help rkt users if the signing and key generation were done by buildah.  Rkt is agnostic about how your distribute your image, but given it's discovery and download specs are so elegant (turns out the last twenty years of web infrastructure **is** sufficient to securely download zip files and the information to verify what you have downloaded), it means you can distribute via github, S3/cloudfront, sourceforge etc. etc.  Each of those distribution points have various tooling to ease uploading whatever buildah produces.

Right now I'm set and have things in place, so I'm good;)
 I can see several open source projects that would benefit from being able to distributed signed containers, e.g. Julia's data science container stacks.
Of course do users want to trust that signature? etc.  That is a separate issue, which rkt also addresses in the most elegant and authoritative way - it asks you.

> turns out the last twenty years of web infrastructure is sufficient to securely download zip files

Do note that the docker/distribution protocol is pretty much equivalent in this respect; the complexity arises only when you want to have a protocol to _update_ the storage (for which the “twenty years of web infrastructure” does not really have a widespread reliable solution).

And I’d hardly call parsing HTML as an essential part of the discovery mechanism elegant.

> Of course do users want to trust that signature? etc. That is a separate issue, which rkt also addresses in the most elegant and authoritative way - it asks you.

Image pull time is not a great time to make decisions about authorized keys; that should happen well in advance, when the user is not under a time pressure to get something else done and likely to just say “yes”. Ideally, by distributing a site-wide config file through a config management system.


Anyway, this is all moot until someone is interested to actually write all that code…

@mtrmac, point taken. As I said I don't pretend to understand the use cases (business models) that are in mind for the spec.  If downloading OCI containers is just using the web standards for zip files, then I'd imagine uploading them is the same ;)  Of course there are newer interesting update mechanisms, such as git-LFS, etc. but then the spec would be define implementation details.

As I said I don't pretend to understand what use cases are driving the specs.

Rkt uses std key-trust mechanisms so you can preauthorize the keys - its been awhile but from memory this is what I did.   We agree, you can't stop people electrocuting themselves and that is no reason to turn off electricity.

I reopened this in the following spirit, before the code comes the idea and the use case.  I've shared my ideas and the use case.  

It seems the project doesn't yet reject the use case.
Since RKT is being deemphasized, I am going to close this issue.
